narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68% chance that you would not pass—well above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the model zeroed in on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out as the strongest drivers of this outcome. Most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, as it placed you well below the typical 6th decile average among those who passed. Your Year 1 decile of 1 had a similar but slightly lesser impact, again signaling lower early performance compared to the 6th decile norm for successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing since it aligns closely with the majority of admitted students who succeeded. Compared to typical accepted applicants—who often have LSAT scores around 37 and undergraduate GPAs near 3.24—your scores of 32 and 2.90 respectively didn't carry as much weight in this specific decision, though they contribute to the overall picture alongside your full-time study plans. These top three factors together tipped the scales toward rejection, reflecting how the model weighs early and later undergraduate performance heavily against bar success odds. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid family background and commitment to full-time study. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or higher, and focus on boosting your GPA through targeted coursework or post-bac programs that could improve your decile rankings. Building a stronger academic track record now could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at other programs. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact areas—your next steps could rewrite this story.
